terms.definition: The degree to which parts of a system remain internally aligned so that the system can function as a unified whole.
The progressive loss of internal alignment across multiple sub-systems, leading to fragmentation and loss of coordinated function.
Uncontrolled external perturbations that disrupt the phase alignment of a system’s internal states.
A feedback process in which the breakdown of one domain accelerates failures in connected domains.
A pre-specified empirical criterion that, if met, falsifies a given hypothesis within the framework.
A systemic tipping point at which small parameter changes lead to qualitatively different macroscopic states.
In this context, the tendency for disorder to increase more readily than order, making destructive processes easier than constructive ones.
Capable of being tested by empirical observation in such a way that the proposition could, in principle, be proven false.
